text: The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection, utilizing the tethering effect of the lateral phlegmon to assist in elevating the tissue, with the retroperitoneum appearing uninvolved.
The surgeon elevates the superior hemorrhoidal vessel using a ligature device and enters the retro-rectal space, navigating through inflamed tissues.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter and proceeds to ligate the superior hemorrhoidal vessel. Due to the challenging medial dissection caused by the phlegmon on the opposite side, attention is directed towards the phlegmon attached to the left dome of the bladder and anterior abdominal wall, which is significantly adherent.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to navigate the inflamed tissue, attempting to enter a natural plane more cephalad. The dissection progresses deeper into the retroperitoneum than intended, with the surgeon identifying the correct plane at a specific location.

The surgeon attempts to re-enter the retroperitoneal plane by dissecting at the current level, carefully separating the white line from the retroperitoneum and mesocolon to protect retroperitoneal structures such as the ureter.
The surgeon switches the camera to ICG mode, aiming to visualize the left ureter, which should appear green if approached closely.
The surgeon observes the left adnexa being drawn into the disease process, with the round ligament on the left side visibly involved.

The surgeon performs a left salpingectomy and oophorectomy en bloc with the specimen. The left ureter is visible, highlighted by green fluorescence from ICG, confirming its location and allowing for safe separation of the phlegmon.

The surgeon uses ICG to facilitate the dissection, ensuring the ureter is visible and safe from injury. The remaining attachments of the distal sigmoid colon and upper rectum are carefully dissected from the lateral sidewall, freeing these structures.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal dissection of the proximal mesentery to facilitate a fan and steel extraction, ensuring the specimen can be removed without tearing. The proximal conduit is positioned to reach the mid rectum for anastomosis. The distal mesentery is then dissected.

The surgeon navigates through the inflamed upper rectum and mesentery, which are visibly affected by previous perforation and chronic adherence to the cavity. The mesentery appears swollen and red as the surgeon attempts to achieve hemostasis during dissection.

The surgeon confirms the blood supply using ICG, which appears adequate. The distal mesentery is divided in preparation for extracorporeal extraction of the specimen. The specimen is extracted through a four-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision, and an anvil is placed at the extraction site.

The surgeon prepares for EEA anastomosis using laparoscopic guidance to navigate the stapler through intestinal folds, ensuring the spike reaches the apex. The anvil is attached, and the mesentery is checked for twists. The procedure involves a Baker anastomosis, which is a side-to-end connection.

The surgeon checks for twisting in the mesentery and ensures there is no tension. The stapler is withdrawn, and a leak test is performed, showing a successful anastomosis. A proctoscope is passed through the anastomosis, confirming it is widely patent, concluding the operation.
